subject: ap-chemistry
title: Intermolecular Forces, Vapor Pressure & Ideal Gas Law

parts.prompt: Based on VSEPR theory, predict the geometry around the atom in compound Y.
parts.scoring_notes: Compound Y contains a Si atom with 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs — tetrahedral geometry.

scoring_points.description: For the correct molecular geometry (tetrahedral): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Compound Y has a Si central atom with 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs. By VSEPR theory, 4 electron domains → tetrahedral electron geometry and tetrahedral molecular geometry.

wrong_examples: Square planar — that requires 4 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs.
Pyramidal — that requires 3 bonding pairs + 1 lone pair.
common_traps: Distinguish electron geometry from molecular geometry; both are tetrahedral when there are 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 01: For the correct answer: Tetrahedral.

sample_responses.response: Tetrahedral. The Si atom in compound Y has 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs. By VSEPR theory, 4 electron domains give tetrahedral electron geometry and tetrahedral molecular geometry.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies VSEPR to Si with 4 bonding domains and no lone pairs, giving tetrahedral geometry. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Square planar. Si has 4 bonds arranged in a plane.
sample_responses.commentary: Square planar geometry requires 4 bonding pairs plus 2 lone pairs (6 electron domains total, as in XeF₄). With only 4 electron domains and no lone pairs, the geometry is tetrahedral. No credit.

parts.prompt:
A student claims that compound Y has a higher boiling point than that of compound X
because compound Y has stronger London dispersion forces. Do you agree or disagree?
Justify your answer.
82°C
parts.scoring_notes: Compound X contains C (smaller, fewer electrons), compound Y contains Si (larger, more electrons). Si has more occupied electron shells and a larger, more polarizable electron cloud → stronger LDF → higher boiling point. Agree with student's claim.

scoring_points.description: For 'agree' with a valid explanation referencing Si's larger/more polarizable electron cloud: 1 point

required_elements: Si has more occupied electron shells than C, giving compound Y a larger, more polarizable electron cloud.
Larger polarizability → stronger London dispersion forces → higher boiling point.
alternatives.correct_example: Agree. Compound Y contains Si, which has 3 occupied electron shells (vs. 2 for C in compound X). The larger Si atom has a greater number of electrons in a more spatially extended cloud, making it more polarizable. Stronger London dispersion forces between Y molecules require more energy to overcome, resulting in a higher boiling point.

wrong_examples: Disagreeing — the student's claim is correct.
Arguing C has more electrons than Si — Si (Z=14) has more electrons than C (Z=6).
common_traps: Molar mass alone is not sufficient justification; must connect to electron cloud size and polarizability.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 02: For the correct answer and a valid justification: Agree. Compound Y has a larger, more polarizable electron cloud because the Si atom has more occupied electron shells than the C atom, giving compound Y stronger London dispersion forces and a higher boiling point than compound X.

sample_responses.response: Agree. The Si atom in compound Y has more occupied electron shells than the C atom in compound X, making compound Y's electron cloud larger and more polarizable. Stronger London dispersion forces between Y molecules require more energy to overcome, resulting in a higher boiling point.
sample_responses.commentary: Agrees with the student and correctly connects the larger, more polarizable Si electron cloud to stronger LDF and higher boiling point. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Agree. Compound Y has a higher molar mass than compound X, so it has a higher boiling point.
sample_responses.commentary: While higher molar mass does correlate with stronger LDF, this response does not explain why — it must connect to electron cloud size and polarizability (more electron shells → larger cloud → stronger LDF). Molar mass alone is insufficient. No credit.

parts.prompt:
An equimolar mixture of the two compounds is heated. When the mixture reaches ,
which compound will have the higher vapor pressure? Justify your answer.
198°C
parts.scoring_notes: At 198°C (471 K), compound X (with C, lower boiling point ~82°C) has already boiled and is entirely gas-phase if unconfined; but in a sealed system both are gases. Compound X has weaker IMF, so at any temperature its vapor pressure is higher than Y. Accept compound X with reasoning about weaker LDF.

scoring_points.description: For identifying compound X with correct justification based on weaker IMF: 1 point

required_elements: Compound X has the higher vapor pressure at 198°C.
Compound X has weaker London dispersion forces (smaller C atom, less polarizable) than compound Y (larger Si atom), so molecules of X escape the liquid phase more readily.
alternatives.correct_example: Compound X has the higher vapor pressure at 198°C. Compound X contains C and has weaker London dispersion forces than compound Y (which contains Si). Because the intermolecular forces in X are weaker, molecules of X require less thermal energy to escape into the vapor phase, giving X a higher vapor pressure at any given temperature.

wrong_examples: Compound Y has a higher vapor pressure — Y has stronger LDF and therefore a lower vapor pressure at a given temperature.
Claiming both have equal vapor pressure — they have different molar masses and IMF strengths.
common_traps: Higher boiling point corresponds to lower vapor pressure at a given temperature (stronger IMF).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 03: For the correct answer and a valid justification. Accept: Compound X. Compound X has weaker intermolecular forces than compound Y, so molecules of X are more likely to be in the gas phase at 82°C and would therefore have a higher vapor pressure. OR: At 82°C, compound X has reached its boiling point, but compound Y has not. Therefore, the proportion of X molecules in the vapor phase would be much greater than that of compound Y, giving compound X the higher vapor pressure.

sample_responses.response: Compound X has the higher vapor pressure at 82°C. Compound X has weaker London dispersion forces (C is smaller and less polarizable than Si), so X molecules escape the liquid phase more easily. Weaker intermolecular forces → higher vapor pressure at any given temperature.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies X and explains the connection between weaker LDF and higher vapor pressure. Matches CB's first accepted explanation. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Compound Y has the higher vapor pressure because it has a higher boiling point and therefore more energy.
sample_responses.commentary: A higher boiling point means stronger IMF and lower vapor pressure at a given temperature — the reasoning is backwards. Compound X, with weaker LDF, has higher vapor pressure. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The mixture is heated to in a sealed, rigid 12.5 L container, at which point both
substances are gases and the total pressure in the container is 2.30 atm. Calculate the number
of moles of gas particles in the container.
parts.scoring_notes: Use ideal gas law: $n = PV/RT$ where T = 198°C = 471 K.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated number of moles using $PV = nRT$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using T = 198 K instead of 471 K — must convert °C to K.
Using R = 8.314 J/mol·K with atm·L units — creates inconsistent units.
common_traps: Always convert temperature to Kelvin: T(K) = T(°C) + 273.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 04: For the correct calculated value: $PV = nRT$; $n = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{(0.280\text{ atm})(12.457\text{ L})}{(0.08206\text{ L·atm/(mol·K)})(471\text{ K})} = 0.744\text{ mol}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly converts 198°C to 471 K, applies the ideal gas law with R = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K), and obtains the correct moles. Earns the point.
Used T = 198 (°C) instead of converting to Kelvin (471 K). Temperature in the ideal gas law must always be in Kelvin. No credit.
